sections.title: Quant. vs qual; Hard vs soft
sections.summary: Quantitative and qualitative evidence are complementary. Use qualitative methods to understand why and quantitative methods to confirm behavior at scale.
coreConcepts: Soft/qualitative evidence explains motivations and reasoning.
Hard/quantitative evidence validates behavior under real-world conditions.
Trust behavior signals when qualitative and quantitative findings conflict.
pitfalls: Self-reported answers can be biased and hypothetical.
Hard evidence can show what changed but not why.
selectionGuidance: Start with qualitative methods to sharpen assumptions.
Then use quantitative methods to validate impact.
Layer methods when stakes are high.

sections.title: 5 overarching categories
sections.summary: Grouping methods by category helps teams pick a stronger test mix and reduce blind spots.
coreConcepts: Pick category first, then select specific methods.
Each category has predictable strengths and weaknesses.
A layered category mix improves evidence quality.
pitfalls: Over-relying on one familiar category creates blind spots.
Desktop research quality depends heavily on source quality and relevance.
selectionGuidance: Combine at least two categories for important assumptions.
Use categories to balance speed, depth, and evidence quality.
Escalate toward real-world behavior methods for stronger confirmation.

sections.title: By goal/product phase
sections.summary: Method selection should follow your current product phase and learning goal.
coreConcepts: Problem-space work prioritizes context and motivations.
Value proposition work tests resonance and messaging.
Early and mature product phases require stronger behavioral evidence.
pitfalls: Using late-stage quantitative tests too early can waste time.
Skipping context methods can produce solution bias.
selectionGuidance: Align tests with phase: discovery, value-prop, early product, mature product.
Move from exploratory signal to confirmatory signal as confidence grows.

sections.title: Ideal Customer Profile (ICP)
sections.summary: Strict ICP definition and participant filtering are essential for usable evidence.
coreConcepts: Only recruit and count participants who match your ICP.
Wrong-participant feedback creates false confidence.
pitfalls: Free-user behavior may not reflect paid-user economics in PLG contexts.
selectionGuidance: Define exclusion criteria before running tests.
Segment findings by ICP fit before making decisions.

sections.title: Testing assumptions, not ideas
sections.summary: Test atomic assumptions (not whole ideas) to isolate what is true or false and reduce wasted effort.
coreConcepts: Ideas bundle multiple assumptions (desirability, feasibility, viability, usability, ethics).
Single-assumption tests isolate failure points.
One risky assumption can invalidate multiple idea variants.
pitfalls: Whole-idea tests produce ambiguous failure signals.
Skipping low-fidelity tests increases cost without clarity.
selectionGuidance: Run low-cost tests first, then escalate fidelity.

sections.title: Isolating your point of (MVP) failure with AMPIE
sections.summary: AMPIE helps diagnose where an MVP failed so teams can run targeted follow-up tests.
coreConcepts: A: Audience fit
M: Messaging clarity
P: Problem importance
I: Idea quality
E: Execution quality
pitfalls: Treating a failed MVP metric as a single undifferentiated failure.
Skipping post-test diagnosis leads to random iteration.
selectionGuidance: Diagnose AMPIE dimension first, then choose targeted methods.
Use qualitative follow-up to explain behavioral outcomes.

sections.title: Reducing risk of false positives/negatives
sections.summary: High-stakes assumptions require layered testing across methods and categories.
coreConcepts: Single tests are often insufficient for high-risk decisions.
Method triangulation reduces false signals.
pitfalls: Making high-impact decisions from one weak signal.
Going straight to quantitative tests before fixing obvious qualitative issues.
selectionGuidance: Run soft/qualitative tests before hard/quantitative tests.
Confirm with real-world behavior when possible.
